coredata.dc:title: Study of ion transmission in an electrolyte of graphene quantum dots under ultraviolet light
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yingping
Tian, Zhen
Lian, Jie
Zhang, Haixia
Wang, Xiaomin
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be used in electrolytes to improve ion transmission and conductivity, but this practice needs further investigation to improve its efficiency. This work focuses on the study of ion transmission in GQD electrolytes under ultraviolet light. A hydrothermal method was used to synthesise GQDs by using three-dimensional graphenes (3DGs) as a carbon precursor. Microstructural characterization showed that the GQDs were distributed at a distance of 10–20 nm, and that the surface of each GQD consisted solely of functional hydroxy groups. In addition, the GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescent behaviour under excitation wavelengths, with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 360 nm and 452 nm, respectively. The electron transfer of GQDs was studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) under ultraviolet irradiation (the GQDs were used as an electrolyte). Our results reveal that GQDs have good absorbance under ultraviolet light, and facilitate the decomposition of electron-hole pairs and improve electron transfer rates in solution. These results are attributed to the fact that the surfaces of GQDs only contain hydroxy functional groups. The number of H+ ions increases when GQDs are ionized under acidic conditions, resulting in the acceleration of ion transmission. Our findings have important implications for subjects such as water purification, electrolyte additives, and the simulation of ion transmission.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Electrolyte
Ion transmission
Ultraviolet light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of 3DGs; (b) TEM image of 3DGs; (c) Raman spectrum of 3DGs.
TEM images of GQD in different reaction time: (a) 6 h, (b) 8 h, (c) 10 h, (d) 12 h.
AFM images of GQDs in different reaction time (a) 6 h, (b) 8 h, (c) 10 h, (d)12 h. The inset of (a, b, c, d) are the height profile along the line in (a, b, c, d).
PL spectra of GQD in different reaction time under the same excitation wavelength (λ = 360 nm) and the same concentration (20 mg/mL).
Nyquist plots of GQDs in different reaction time at the same concentration of 20 mg/mL, Inset: Impedance of the samples in the high-frequency region.
(a) Raman spectrum of the 10 h GQDs and 3DGs; (b) FTIR spectrum of the 10 h GQDs and 3DGs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs. The inset shows the photograph of aqueous dispersions of GQDs under UV (365 nm) illumination; (b) PL spectra of GQDs solution under different excitation wavelength.

(a) Nyquist plots of S-GQDs and J-GQDs at a concentration of 2, 5, 10, 20 and 40 mg/mL in irradiation. Inset: Impedance of the samples in the high-frequency region. (b) Nyquist plots of S-GQDs at a concentration of 2, 5, 10, 20 and 40 mg/mL in irradiation. Inset: Impedance of the samples in the high-frequency region. (c)Nyquist plots of J-GQDs at a concentration of 2, 5, 10, 20 and 40 mg/mL in irradiation. Inset: Impedance of the samples in the high-frequency region.
(a) Resistances of S-GQDs and J-GQDs at different concentration under illumination; (b) Slopes of S-GQDs and J-GQDs at different concentration under illumination.
Concentration of S-GQDs and J-GQDs electrolytes.
Size and thickness of GQDs at different reaction time.
Resistance and slope of GQDs at different reaction time.
Resistances and slopes of S-GQDs and J-GQDs at different concentration in illumination.

head.ce:title: Study of ion transmission in an electrolyte of graphene quantum dots under ultraviolet light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Energy Material, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Energy Material

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 030024, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be used in electrolytes to improve ion transmission and conductivity, but this practice needs further investigation to improve its efficiency. This work focuses on the study of ion transmission in GQD electrolytes under ultraviolet light. A hydrothermal method was used to synthesise GQDs by using three-dimensional graphenes (3DGs) as a carbon precursor. Microstructural characterization showed that the GQDs were distributed at a distance of 10–20 nm, and that the surface of each GQD consisted solely of functional hydroxy groups. In addition, the GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescent behaviour under excitation wavelengths, with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 360 nm and 452 nm, respectively. The electron transfer of GQDs was studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) under ultraviolet irradiation (the GQDs were used as an electrolyte). Our results reveal that GQDs have good absorbance under ultraviolet light, and facilitate the decomposition of electron-hole pairs and improve electron transfer rates in solution. These results are attributed to the fact that the surfaces of GQDs only contain hydroxy functional groups. The number of H+ ions increases when GQDs are ionized under acidic conditions, resulting in the acceleration of ion transmission. Our findings have important implications for subjects such as water purification, electrolyte additives, and the simulation of ion transmission.

ce:section.ce:para: GQDs, are composed of a quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterial; each GQD is smaller than 100 nm in diameter. They have recently become the focus of much interest in the field of fluorescent carbon materials because they exhibit several unique properties, including the quantum confinement effect, their large specific surface area, edge effects, excellent photostability, highly tuneable photoluminescence (PL) property, chemical inertness, surface functionalization, doping, and unique photoelectric transmission characteristics. These key merits therefore provide unprecedented opportunities for applications such as cell imaging, fluorescent probes, biosensors, and supercapacitors [1–3]. To date, two methods have been established to obtain GQDs: bottom-up and top-down. The bottom–up method involves stepwise solution chemistry along with a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method that features pyrolysis or carbonization of small organic molecules. The top–down method is instead based around acidic oxidation; carbon, graphene, and soot are used as starting materials, these undergo strong acid treatment, with reduced graphene oxide (GO) sheets being used as a precursors (through hydrothermal or solvothermal methods, electrochemical exfoliation, and microwave irradiation methods) [4–6]. Li et al. [7] used fructose and hydrochloric acid as precursors, and then successfully prepared chlorine-doped GQDs (Cl-GQDs) via a facile hydrothermal method. Cl-GQDs were the first carbon-based fluorescent material with a fixed size capable of emitting a variety of colours. Hu et al. [8] developed a hydrothermal process to prepare N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) from GO. They demonstrated that the N-GQDs possessed stronger absorption bands of oxygen-related groups than GO, and achieved quantum yield for N-GQDs of 24.6%. Zhang et al. [9] prepared GQDs via a microwave method, using aspartic acid (Asp) and NH4HCO3 as their source materials. They demonstrated that the hydrophilicity and stability of GQDs improved when in an aqueous solution, attributing this improvement to the oxygen-rich property of the GQDs. Furthermore, complexation between phenolic hydroxyl groups present on GQDs and Fe3+ ions resulted in electron transfer to the d orbital of Fe3+, which accounts for the phenomenon of Fe3+ having a strong fluorescence quenching effect on GQDs. Liu et al. [10] reported a method to fabricate a GQDs–Cu2O/BPM composite (BPM=bipolar membrane). They found that GQDs could effectually segregate photo-generated electron–hole pairs and facilitate electron transfer, further, enhancing the efficiency of photoelectric conversion. It has been verified that the bonds of water molecules are relaxed by the induced polarization effect, which is attributed to GQDs with many carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces. It has also been shown that the hydrophilicity of GQDs–Cu2O/BPM composites can be improved with the help of the hydrophilic groups of GQDs. Arundithi et al. [11] demonstrated an electrochemical method for synthesising GQDs from 3D graphene grown by chemical vapour deposition (CVD). The obtained GQDs, when functionalized with 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BMIMPF6), could be employed to optically detect Fe3+ because of complexation between the imidazole ring of BMIM+ and Fe3+ ions. Ma et al. [12] obtained GQDs from GO that could be applied to detect chlorite in spiked tap water and living cells, owing to an efficient electron transfer process from GQDs to oxidized o-phenylenediamine (OPD). They showed that carboxyl groups present on GQDs could be activated (each group effectively responded to one amino group from OPD), and also demonstrated that, OPD had good selectivity of ClO−, which they attributed to specific oxidation reactions. Hosseini et al. [13] synthesized GQDs using GO as a raw material. They found that H2 is dissociated into H+ ions on the GQDs due to their surface groups, edges, and defects, which exchange protons with the GQDs. Consequently, Hosseini et al. [13] were able to reduce sensor resistance when sensing humidity. The process of doping different elements in the process of synthesising GQDs can tailor them to a wide variety of different fields, and the rich source of functional groups on the surface of GQDs also increases their versatility. However, the diversity of functional groups on the surface of GQDs also reduces their ionic conductivity and limits their application to a certain degree. Zhang et al. [14] proposed that GQDs derived from GO with large numbers of functional groups such as -COOH and -OH on their surfaces could serve as electrolytes for supercapacitors. They demonstrated that the ion-donating ability and ionic conductivity of the GQDs could be improved dramatically merely by using KOH to neutralize their acidic oxygen-bearing functional groups. Understanding how to control the functional groups of GQDs will allow for improvements in the efficiency of ion transmission in solution and will expand the fields that GQDs could be applied to.

In this paper, we report an acid-free and non-toxic hydrothermal method to prepare GQDs from three-dimensional graphene grown by CVD; this makes it possible to obtain an electrolyte containing GQDs at different concentrations. We then investigate the ion transport characteristics of the elecreolyte containing GQDs under ultraviolet light, through electrochemical measurement, to track the material in the solution and simulate ion transmission rates in the electrolyte.

The 3DGs were synthesized at carbon nanomaterial laboratory, and purchased ethanol and NaOH from Tianjing Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd., China. All reagents were of analytical grade, and were used without further purification. We used deionized (DI) water purified through a Millipore system throughout the experiment, and purchased dialysis membranes from Spectra/Pro Biotec.

The GQDs were obtained via the solvothermal treatment of 3DGs [15]. In a typical procedure, 1 mg 3DGs and 30 mg NaOH were mixed with 50 mL ethanol in a beaker and stirred quickly for five minutes. The mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and then heated at 180 ℃ for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, unreacted 3DGs were filtered out using a mixed cellulose ester membrane with 0.22 μm-size-pores (Millipore). The remaining solution (yellow in colour) was dialyzed with an 8000–14400 Da dialysis bag containing deionized water for 48 h to remove excess NaOH and ethanol. The solution (now light yellow) was then freeze-dried, to obtain GQDs in powder form. The GQDs were then re-dispersed in water at different concentrations, before being divided into two subsamples. The pH of these subsamples were adjusted to 6.0 and 8.0, using 1 M HCl solution and 1 M NaOH solution respectively; herein we refer to the pH 6.0 GQDs as S-GQDs and the pH 8.0 GQDs as J-GQDs. The methods used to prepare the electrolytes are shown in Table 1.

After exposing the electrolytes to ultraviolet light for 10 min, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, China). Impedance spectroscopy was performed using a three-electrode cell, with platinum wires being used as the working electrode and counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode being used as the reference electrode; measurements were performed at open circuit potential (OCP) with a sinusoidal signal of 5 mV and a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz.

The morphologies of GQDs were characterized using a JSM-7600F field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (JEOL, Japan). A SPA-300HV atomic force microscopy (AFM) (JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize the thicknesses of the samples. A JEM-2010 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize the microstructures of the samples. The Raman spectrum was obtained by a CRM200 Raman spectrometer (WITEC, Germany) at an emission wavelength of 514 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs was measured using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor, Germany), and the PL spectra of the GQDs dispersed in deionized water were obtained using a FluroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan). Absorption was measured using a U-3900 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

Fig. 1a shows an SEM image of the CVD-grown graphene. The network and porous structure of the graphene has been preserved following the removal of the Ni template, without cracking or collapsing. Several defects (in the form of wrinkles and cracks) appear on the skeleton, which may have been caused by the Ni template and the stress concentration during the preparation process, or by the removal process [16]. Fig. 1b depicts a TEM image of the obtained 3DGs, confirming their flat surface and thin structure. The process of Raman spectroscopy was able to accurately characterise the 3DGs; the typical D (∼ 1352 cm−1), G (∼ 1581 cm−1), and 2D (∼ 2728 cm−1) peaks are characteristic of 3DGs (Fig. 1c). The ratio of the integrated peak area of the 2D band compared to the G band (∼ 0.53) indicates that the layer has few characteristic [17].

TEM images show that different reaction time produced GQDs of varying diameters ( Fig. 2). The diameters of the GQDs produced after 6 h ranged from 45 nm to 55 nm (Fig. 2a), whereas the diameters of those produced after 8 h ranged from 20 nm to 30 nm. By the time the reaction reached 10 h, the diameters of the GQDs being produced had decreased to 10 nm to 20 nm (Fig. 2c), and their diameters decreased further after 12 h, to 3.0–5.0 nm (Fig. 2d). It is clear therefore that preparation time has a significant effect on the sizes of the GQDs being produced. There are several reasons which could explain the observed morphology of the GQDs: (1) it is inevitable that some defects and cracks will develop on the 3DGs during their growth, etching, and transfer processes through CVD; (2) the defects and edges of 3DGs can be corroded by NaOH in the ethanol solution, and the properties of Na+ ions are such that they are conducive to insertion between layers or cracks in the 3DGs; (3) high temperatures and pressures may further enhance the interaction between the 3DGs and NaOH in the ethanol solution [18]. The edges and cracks of the 3DGs are the areas most susceptible to damage, resulting in an uneven distribution of GQDs [19].

The thickness of the GQDs was characterized using atomic force microscopy (AFM). It can be seen that the height distribution of the peaks for the 6 h GQDs ranged from 1 nm to 1.7 nm ( Fig. 3a), revealing that these GQDs have 3–5 graphene layers [13]. The height distribution of the peaks of the 8 h GQDs ranged from 1 nm to 1.5 nm, also implying they have approximately 3–5 graphene layers (Fig. 3b). The height distribution of the peaks of the 10 h GQDs ranged from 0.4 nm to 0.85 nm however (Fig. 3c), revealing that these GQDs have 2–3 graphene layers [20], and the height distribution of the peaks from the 12 h GQDs ranged from 0.35 nm to 0.5 nm(Fig. 3d), implying these GQDs consist of monolayer graphene [21]. The sizes and thicknesses of the GQDs at different reaction time are shown in Table 2.

PL spectra of GQDs at different reaction times (carried out under the same excitation wavelengths), show that fluorescence intensity intensified when the reaction time increased from 6 to 8 h, reached its maximum intensity at 10 h, and then started to decrease ( Fig. 4). This phenomenon could be caused by the size of GQDs, or by the GQDs aggregating after reaching a certain size, as aggregates have either a lower fluorescence intensity or quench the fluorescence of neighbouring GQDs [22].

The Nyquist plots of GQDs at different reaction time (all with a concentration of 20 mg/mL) have no semicircle in the high-frequency region ( Fig. 5); they instead show a straight line in the low-frequency region. These two regions, correspond to electrochemical and mass transfer processes, respectively [23]. The 10 h GQDs electrolyte exhibits the smallest R value of the four (11.28 Ω), and its line is closest to vertical in the low-frequency region ( Table 3), thereby indicating that this electrolyte is the best candidate for supercapacitors, due to its low resistance and faster charge transfer [24].

The faster charge transfer performance of the 10 h GQDs electrolyte may be attributed to the following features in its structure. The 10 h GQDs, which are 10–20 nm in diameter, provide the lowest resistance in the electrolyte, and 10–20 nm is the most suitable size. Though the 12 h GQDs have the smallest diameters (3–5 nm), they may be aggregated, and they have lower light absorption under ultraviolet illumination, which influences the resistance and ion transmission rates in the electrolyte. Furthermore, the 10 h GQDs have 2–3 layers of graphene, and therefore retain the part of graphene structure that can enhance electron transport during the charge/discharge process because of its high specific area and conductivity. This explains why the ion transmission rate of the 10 h GQDs electrolyte is higher than that for the 6 h GQDs and 8 h GQDs electrolytes [25].

Raman spectroscopy has been widely used to characterize carbon, and it is an effective method to examine the quality of graphene [26]. When comparing the Raman spectrum of GQDs and 3DGs, D and G bands of ∼ 1352 cm−1 and ∼ 1581 cm−1 respectively are characteristic of 3DGs, whereas GQDs have typical D and G bands of ∼ 1274 cm−1, ∼ 1478 cm−1 respectively ( Fig. 6a). The D band is associated with disordered layers and edges of the carbon layers, and the G band is caused by the in-plane vibration of the symmetric sp 2 C-C [27]. In the Raman spectrum of graphene, the D band is usually not obvious. Comparing the Raman spectra of GQDs and 3DGs, it can be seen that the intensity of the D band of the GQDs is obviously enhanced, due to the destruction of the edge symmetry of the 3DGs during their preparation. In addition, the carbon structure of the GQDs becomes disordered in the solvothermal process, resulting in the broadening of the G band. Compared to the D and G bands of the 3DGs, the D and G bands of the GQDs have shifted considerably. There are two possible reasons for this shift: (1) amorphization of graphite may have occurred during to the fractionation of sp 2 carbon from 3DGs to GQDs [26], or (2) the GQDs are flawed due to defects in the 3DGs, as well as the higher disorder in the edges of GQDs [28].

FTIR of GQDs and 3DGs shows that GQDs exhibit the characteristic triplet peaks of C–H asymmetric and symmetric vibration (at 2928 cm−1 and 2357 cm−1), whose peaks (at 3445 cm−1, 1628 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1) can be attributed to characteristic bands of the O-H stretching vibrations (Fig. 6b). The band at 1145 cm−1 is attributed to C˭O stretching vibration. These results indicate that the 3DGs have no functional groups on their surfaces, and that the surfaces of the GQDs only have hydroxy functional groups. The presence of these hydroxy groupsexplains why GQDs exhibit good hydrophilicity and stability in an aqueous system, which demonstrates their great potential in applications such as cell imaging and water purification.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs shows a blue colour ( Fig. 7a). The spectrum of GQDs exhibit a typical absorption at 262 nm and a shoulder peak in the range of 304 nm, which differed from C-dots and is possibly attributable to the n-π* transition of C˭O bonds. This analysis also confirms that GQDs’ surfaces contained hydroxy functional groups [29]. The inset of Fig. 7a is an optical image of the GQDs solution under UV irradiation. The solution shows blue fluorescence, confirming that there has been no precipitation of GQDs even after the GQD solution has been maintained at room temperature for a month, implying that the solution can maintain stability and fluorescence.

The emission wavelengths of the GQDs exhibit an almost excitation-dependent photoluminescence at an excitation wavelength of 320–420 nm (Fig. 7b). This excitation- dependent PL behaviour probably mainly comes from the internal GQD structure (inter-band transition) [30]. GQDs emit blue luminescence when they are excited by a 365 nm UV beam, and display maximum excitation under emission wavelengths of 360 and 452 nm, respectively. Therefore, when GQDs aqueous solution is used as an electrolyte, the UV irradiation can be used to ensure maximum absorbance of the GQDs, improving the electrolyte's efficiency in the hole-transport material of ion transmission.

EIS is a powerful tool for obtaining information on the chemical reactions taking place in both the reaction electrode material and the electrolyte [31]. Our impedance spectra clearly show that the value of the high-frequency intercept with the real axis in S-GQDs curve is smaller than that in J-GQDs curve, and that the slope between the high-frequency and the real axis in S-GQDs curve is larger than that in J-GQDs curve ( Fig. 8a). According to the EIS measurements, the point of intersection between the high-frequency and the real axis represents the equivalent series resistance(R), which is associated with the sum of the electrolyte solution resistance, the intrinsic resistance of the active material, and the contact resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Capacitance (C) is used to express charge accumulation mechanisms from the semiconductor to the solution interface [32,33]. From the magnified high-frequency region in the inset of Fig. 8a, we can see that the R values in S-GQDs are smaller than those in J-GQDs at each concentration. The smaller the equivalent series resistance is, the smaller the energy dissipation of the system is. There is no doubt that the energy dissipation is smaller in S-GQDs. In particular, the absence of a semicircle in the high frequency region in the plot, implies fast charge transfer [34]. In addition, the curve of the S-GQDs is nearly parallel to the imaginary axis in the low frequency region, indicating fast charge transfer and ideal capacitive behaviour. It has been demonstrated that the closer the line is to being vertical, the more closely the supercapacitor behaves as an ideal capacitor [35,36]. Our results revealed that the ion transmission of the electrolyte containing S-GQDs was better than that containing J-GQDs electrolyte, when comparing with equivalent series resistance and slope. The R values of S-GQDs and J-GQDs are decreased with the increasing concentration (Fig. 8b and c). The curve for the S-GQDs plots are closer to the imaginary axis with increasing concentration in the low frequency region (Fig. 8b). However, for the J-GQDs, the trend is exactly the opposite, implying that ion transportion is better in the S-GQDs electrolyte. From the magnified high-frequency region in the inset of Fig. 8b, it can be seen that the S-GQDs electrolytes have very low R values in comparison to the J-GQDs electrolytes at each concentration, implying that there is good contact between the current collector and the electrode material, and that the electrode/electrolyte interface in the S-GQDs electrolyte has a small ohmic resistance [37].

The R values and slopes of the S-GQDs and J-GQDs electrolytes under UV irradiation at different concentrations show that the R values decreased with increasing concentration for both ( Fig. 9, Table 4). However, the ion transmission rate increased with increasing concentration, and reached its maximum value at a concentration of 20 mg/mL, in the S-GQDs electrolyte, but showed the opposite trend for the J-GQDs electrolyte. This is because the GQDs in both cases only contain hydroxy functional groups on their surfaces. GQDs only can release H+ ions when the GQD electrolyte ionized, so the number of H+ ions increases when GQDs are ionized under acidic conditions, accelerating the ion transmission rate. Furthermore, GQDs have better absorbance under UV light when this prepared via this method. Thus, it can extend the light absorption of GQDs under UV light, enhance electron–hole separation, and enhance the quantum-confined bandgap narrowing mechanism [38]. In addition, GQDs can act as a photosensitizer, because of their broad visible light absorption, and because their electrons enter an excited state under UV irradiation [39,40]. This allows the photoinduced electron hole to decompose, and the electron transfer rate can then be accelerated in the GQDs electrolyte. In addition, in the system of electrochemical and photocatalytic behaviour, electrons are photoaccumulated and charge can be screened by electrolyte cations when the electron acceptor is absent. GQDs can release H+ ions when photoinduced, due to them only having hydroxy functional groups on their surfaces [41,42]. The above results demonstrate that the S-GQDs electrolyte is best for ion transmission.

In summary, GQDs with diameters from 10 to 20 nm can be prepared in a hydrothermal process by using 3DGs as a carbon precursor. The obtained GQDs solely contain hydroxy functional groups on their surfaces and exhibit their maximum excitation at 452 nm, with an emission wavelength of 360 nm. EIS results suggest that GQDs have a good absorbance under ultraviolet light, which can facilitate the decomposition of electron hole pairs and improve electron transfer rates in solution. Owing to the fact that the surface of GQDs contain only hydroxy functional groups, the number of H+ ions increases when GQDs are ionized under acidic conditions, resulting in the acceleration of ion transmission in the solution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. U1710256, No. 51572184 and No. 51372160).
